questions.question: What is the main objective of a Virtual Private Database (VPD)?

answers.b: To restrict data access at the row level based on user identity

explanations.a: Encryption is different from row-level access control.
explanations.b: VPD ensures users access only authorized rows.
explanations.c: Performance tuning is not the goal of VPD.
explanations.d: Backups are unrelated to access control.

questions.question: At which level does VPD enforce access control?

explanations.b: Table-level control does not filter rows.
explanations.c: VPD restricts access at the row level.

questions.question: Which SQL command is used to define a row-level security rule?

explanations.a: CREATE TABLE is used to create tables.
explanations.b: CREATE POLICY defines row-level security rules.
explanations.c: Views do not enforce RLS policies.

questions.question: What must be enabled on a table for policies to work?

explanations.a: Triggers are not required for RLS.
explanations.b: Encryption does not control row visibility.
explanations.c: RLS must be enabled to enforce policies.

questions.question: How does VPD decide which rows a user can see?

answers.c: Based on the user’s login identity

explanations.b: Table size does not affect access.
explanations.c: User identity is used to filter rows.
explanations.d: Database name does not control access.

questions.question: Why are multiple users created in a VPD experiment?

answers.b: To test user-based row access control

explanations.a: Load testing is not the purpose.
explanations.b: Different users help verify row-level restrictions.
